past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, managed with oral hypoglycemics and insulin
Hypothyroidism, diagnosed 8 years ago, on levothyroxine replacement
Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, on beta-blocker therapy
Bariatric surgery (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass) 7 years ago
Insomnia, chronic, managed with sleep hygiene measures
family_history: Mother died at age 85 from congestive heart failure
Father died at age 78 from complications of type 2 diabetes
No family history of movement disorders, dementia, or neurological conditions

social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a single-story house, independent with ADLs but reports increasing difficulty with fine motor tasks
social_history.exercise: Used to walk daily but has reduced activity due to unsteadiness over the past 3 months

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 22/30, with deficits in visuospatial/executive function (clock drawing, cube copy), delayed recall (2/5 words), and verbal fluency. Attention and language relatively preserved.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No vertical gaze palsy. Facial expression mildly hypomimic bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations. Palate elevates symmetrically.
neurological_exam.motor: Bilateral bradykinesia with decrement on finger tapping, hand movements, and toe tapping, slightly more prominent on the right. Moderate rigidity at wrists and elbows bilaterally with cogwheel quality on the right. No pyramidal weakness. Tone mildly increased in lower extremities. Stooped posture. Pisa syndrome noted with trunk leaning to the left.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. No cortical sensory deficits. Distal extremities cold and pale bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, ankle 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffman sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. No dysdiadochokinesia beyond that attributable to bradykinesia. Heel-to-shin testing mildly slow but accurate.
neurological_exam.gait: Wide-based gait with reduced arm swing bilaterally. Short, shuffling steps. Difficulty initiating turns. Stooped posture. Positive pull test with retropulsion requiring two steps to recover. No festination. Multiple falls reported over the past 4 months.
neurological_exam.additional: Bilateral jerky resting tremor of the hands at approximately 4-5 Hz, re-emergent with sustained posture. Postural tremor also present. No minipolymyoclonus. No dystonia or chorea. Orthostatic vital signs: supine BP 148/88, standing BP 118/68 after 3 minutes (30 mmHg systolic drop, 20 mmHg diastolic drop). No carotid bruits.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive tremor in the right hand, unsteadiness, and recurrent falls over the past 4 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Delgado is a 69-year-old right-handed woman referred to the Movement Disorders Clinic by her primary care physician for evaluation of progressive motor symptoms over the past 4 months. She first noticed dizziness and a general sense of slowness approximately 4 months ago, followed within weeks by a tremor in her right hand that has gradually worsened. The tremor is most noticeable at rest, such as when her hand is resting on her lap while watching television, and partially improves with intentional movements like reaching for objects. Over the past 3 months, the tremor has also appeared intermittently in the left hand. She reports several episodes of falling, occurring approximately once or twice per week, usually when turning or navigating around furniture. She has not lost consciousness during any fall. Her daughter has noticed that her mother's posture has become increasingly stooped and that she appears to lean to one side when sitting or standing. The patient reports progressive difficulty with handwriting, noting that her letters have become smaller and harder to read. Buttoning shirts and opening jars have become increasingly effortful. She has experienced worsening insomnia, with frequent nighttime awakenings and difficulty returning to sleep. She endorses a depressed mood over the past several months, with decreased interest in her usual activities including gardening and reading. She reports urinary urgency and increased frequency, occasionally not reaching the bathroom in time. She also describes feeling lightheaded upon standing, particularly in the morning or after meals, and has had two near-syncopal episodes. She denies visual hallucinations, acting out dreams, or any symptoms suggestive of REM sleep behavior disorder. She denies obsessive-compulsive behaviors. Her appetite has been stable and weight has been steady.

findings.location: Periventricular and deep white matter, bilateral
findings.size: Multiple small foci, each less than 5 mm

additional_observations: No evidence of cerebellar or pontine atrophy
No hot cross bun sign or putaminal rim sign
No midbrain atrophy or hummingbird sign
Ventricles are proportionate to sulcal atrophy, not suggestive of normal pressure hydrocephalus
No acute infarcts on diffusion-weighted imaging
No basal ganglia signal abnormalities or infarcts
No evidence of mass lesion or enhancement post-contrast
mri.impression: Nonspecific scattered white matter hyperintensities consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic changes, likely related to hypertension and diabetes. Mild age-appropriate cortical atrophy. No structural lesion to account for parkinsonism. No features of multiple system atrophy, progressive supranuclear palsy, or normal pressure hydrocephalus.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low - no strategic basal ganglia infarcts

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with movement disorder evaluation
Consider DaTscan if diagnostic uncertainty persists

findings: No left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 68 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia detected.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG. No cardiac etiology identified for reported near-syncopal episodes. Orthostatic hypotension may be autonomic in nature.

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes mellitus. Patient on metformin and insulin.

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function on current levothyroxine dose. Rules out hypothyroid-related psychomotor slowing.

Additional.clinical_significance: Normal. Rules out B12 deficiency as cause of neurological symptoms.
Mildly elevated, reflecting suboptimal glycemic control. Consistent with known diabetes.

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are unremarkable except for mildly elevated fasting glucose and HbA1c, consistent with known type 2 diabetes mellitus with suboptimal control. CBC, renal function, electrolytes, thyroid function, vitamin B12, and ceruloplasmin are all within normal limits. RPR is non-reactive. No metabolic or toxic etiology identified for the patient's parkinsonian syndrome.

findings.location: Bilateral putamina, greater on the left (contralateral to more symptomatic right side)

signal_characteristics.pattern: Asymmetric reduction in putaminal uptake with relative preservation of caudate uptake bilaterally

additional_observations: Caudate-to-putamen ratio increased bilaterally, consistent with preferential putaminal denervation
Left putamen shows more severe uptake reduction than right, correlating with right-sided predominant symptoms
No completely absent uptake in any region
output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan showing bilateral asymmetric reduction in striatal dopamine transporter binding, with left greater than right putaminal involvement. This pattern is consistent with a neurodegenerative parkinsonian syndrome, most consistent with idiopathic Parkinson's disease given the clinical context. The asymmetry of uptake reduction correlates with the clinically observed right-sided predominance of motor symptoms.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Cannot be excluded by DaTscan alone, but clinical features favor PD

recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical features and levodopa response
Consider levodopa trial to assess therapeutic response

Levodopa Challenge Test.test: Post-levodopa UPDRS-III Score (1 hour after 200 mg levodopa/50 mg carbidopa)
Levodopa Challenge Test.clinical_significance: 42% improvement from baseline, exceeding 30% threshold for a positive response
Positive levodopa response. Supports diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease.

output.interpretation: Positive acute levodopa challenge test with 42% improvement in motor UPDRS score. Significant improvement was noted in bradykinesia and rigidity, with mild improvement in tremor. This robust response strongly supports a diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease and predicts a favorable long-term response to dopaminergic therapy.

Autonomic Reflex Screen.value: Systolic BP drop 32 mmHg at 3 min, 28 mmHg at 10 min
Autonomic Reflex Screen.clinical_significance: Neurogenic orthostatic hypotension confirmed. Heart rate increase only 6 bpm (blunted, also partially attributable to beta-blocker).

Autonomic Reflex Screen.value: Reduced late phase II and absent phase IV overshoot
Autonomic Reflex Screen.clinical_significance: Consistent with mild cardiovascular autonomic dysfunction
Mildly reduced sudomotor function distally, consistent with mild autonomic neuropathy

output.interpretation: Autonomic reflex screen confirms neurogenic orthostatic hypotension with mild cardiovascular autonomic dysfunction. Sudomotor function is mildly reduced distally. The degree of autonomic dysfunction is mild and compatible with Parkinson's disease. Note that concurrent beta-blocker (metoprolol) use may be contributing to the blunted heart rate response and exacerbating orthostatic hypotension. The autonomic findings do not reach the severity typically seen in multiple system atrophy.
abnormal_values_summary: Confirmed neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (32 mmHg systolic drop on tilt)
Blunted heart rate response (partially beta-blocker effect)

Neuropsychological Testing.clinical_significance: Executive dysfunction, consistent with frontal-subcortical pattern

Neuropsychological Testing.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test (delayed recall)
Neuropsychological Testing.clinical_significance: Mild memory retrieval deficit, improves with cueing (suggesting subcortical pattern)

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile shows mild cognitive impairment with a frontal-subcortical pattern: executive dysfunction, reduced verbal fluency, psychomotor slowing, and mild retrieval-type memory deficit that improves with cueing. Language and confrontation naming are preserved. This pattern is characteristic of PD-related mild cognitive impairment (PD-MCI) and differs from the cortical amnestic pattern typical of Alzheimer's disease.
abnormal_values_summary: Executive dysfunction on Trails B and clock drawing

Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Reduced sleep efficiency with frequent awakenings
No REM without atonia. No dream-enactment behaviors observed.
Mildly elevated. Periodic limb movements of sleep are common in Parkinson's disease.

output.interpretation: Polysomnography demonstrates reduced sleep efficiency and total sleep time with frequent arousals, consistent with sleep fragmentation commonly seen in Parkinson's disease. Periodic limb movements are mildly elevated. Importantly, no REM sleep behavior disorder was identified, which is consistent with the patient's history. No significant sleep-disordered breathing.

results.title: Self-injurious Behavior in Parkinsonism: A Rare Association
results.summary: Case report of a 69-year-old woman with atypical parkinsonism presenting with self-mutilating behavior. Discussion of Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, drug-related SIB, and SCA17 as differential considerations.

results.title: Impulse control disorders in Parkinson's disease
results.summary: Review of impulse control disorders in PD including pathological gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive eating, and compulsive buying. Self-injurious behavior is rare but has been associated with dopamine agonist therapy.

results.title: Self-injurious behavior in Lesch-Nyhan disease
results.summary: Review of Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, an X-linked disorder of purine metabolism with HPRT deficiency. Presents in early childhood with hyperuricemia, dystonia, and compulsive self-injury. Not applicable to late-onset presentations.

output.summary: Self-injurious behavior in Parkinson's disease is rare. It is more commonly associated with Lesch-Nyhan syndrome (childhood onset, X-linked), impulse control disorders related to dopamine agonist therapy, or specific genetic parkinsonism variants (e.g., FBXO7 mutations). In idiopathic PD, impulse control disorders are typically related to dopaminergic medication, particularly dopamine agonists. Isolated self-injurious behavior without dopaminergic medication exposure should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses or psychiatric comorbidity.

output.proposed: Carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg three times daily for Parkinson's disease
interactions: Fluoxetine + Levodopa: Fluoxetine (SSRI) may theoretically reduce levodopa efficacy through serotonergic effects on dopaminergic pathways, though clinically significant interaction is uncommon. Monitor for reduced levodopa response.
Metoprolol + Levodopa: Beta-blockers may attenuate some cardiovascular effects. Metoprolol may worsen orthostatic hypotension when combined with levodopa. Monitor blood pressure.
contraindications: Non-selective MAO inhibitors (e.g., phenelzine, tranylcypromine) are contraindicated with levodopa - not applicable here as fluoxetine is an SSRI, not an MAOI
warnings: Monitor for worsening orthostatic hypotension given existing autonomic dysfunction and concurrent metoprolol
Monitor for psychiatric side effects (vivid dreams, hallucinations) given existing depression and fluoxetine use
Insulin requirements may change as activity level improves with PD treatment
output.formulary_status: Preferred - first-line treatment for Parkinson's disease
alternatives: Pramipexole (dopamine agonist) - consider as adjunct or if tremor-predominant, but caution with impulse control disorders
Rasagiline (MAO-B inhibitor) - avoid if patient starts an MAOI; safe with levodopa as adjunct
Amantadine - may help with dyskinesias if they develop

output.query: orthostatic hypotension Parkinson's disease versus multiple system atrophy

results.title: Orthostatic hypotension in Parkinson's disease: frequency, severity and risk factors
results.summary: Orthostatic hypotension occurs in 30-58% of PD patients. It is related to disease duration, severity, and dopaminergic medication use. Mild OH is common in PD and does not necessarily indicate MSA.

results.title: Differentiating MSA from Parkinson's disease
results.summary: In MSA, severe early autonomic failure (OH, urinary retention, erectile dysfunction) is a hallmark, typically appearing within 1-2 years of motor onset. In PD, autonomic dysfunction is usually milder and develops later. Key distinguishing features include poor levodopa response and rapid progression in MSA.

output.summary: Orthostatic hypotension is common in Parkinson's disease (30-58% prevalence) and does not by itself differentiate PD from MSA. However, the severity, timing, and associated features help distinguish them: in MSA, severe autonomic failure occurs early and is disproportionate to motor disease duration, whereas in PD, autonomic dysfunction is typically milder and progressive. A positive levodopa response and slower disease progression favor PD over MSA.

differential.key_distinguishing: Orthostatic hypotension and urinary symptoms raise this consideration, but the degree of autonomic dysfunction is mild relative to motor symptoms, no cerebellar signs, no putaminal or pontine atrophy on MRI, and positive levodopa response argues against MSA
Early falls and postural instability could suggest PSP, but patient has no vertical gaze palsy, no midbrain atrophy (hummingbird sign), tremor is prominent (unusual in PSP), and responds to levodopa

Patient has vascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes), but MRI shows only nonspecific white matter changes without strategic basal ganglia infarcts. Clinical features (asymmetric tremor, cogwheel rigidity) are more consistent with PD

Fluoxetine can rarely cause extrapyramidal symptoms, but presentation is asymmetric with resting tremor and progressive course, inconsistent with drug-induced parkinsonism which is typically symmetric
Patient has gait difficulty and urinary symptoms, but MRI shows no ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy. Tremor and cogwheel rigidity are not features of NPH

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism (vascular lesions, NPH, tumor, MSA/PSP features)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Nonspecific white matter changes without structural lesion, no cerebellar/pontine/midbrain atrophy, no ventriculomegaly

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, B12, RPR, and ceruloplasmin to exclude metabolic and toxic causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results except for known diabetes. Rules out hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, Wilson disease, neurosyphilis

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to confirm presynaptic dopaminergic deficit
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced striatal dopamine transporter uptake, asymmetric, consistent with neurodegenerative parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Perform levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Greater than 30% improvement in UPDRS motor score, supporting PD diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Order ECG to evaluate for cardiac causes of near-syncope and falls
Check drug interactions before initiating levodopa therapy given polypharmacy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No absolute contraindications; monitor for worsening orthostatic hypotension with metoprolol; monitor psychiatric effects with fluoxetine

optimal_actions.action: Consider autonomic testing to characterize severity of orthostatic hypotension and differentiate PD from MSA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild autonomic dysfunction consistent with PD rather than severe dysautonomia of MSA

optimal_actions.action: Order neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive impairment pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Frontal-subcortical pattern consistent with PD-MCI, not cortical Alzheimer's pattern

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism
Order laboratory tests to rule out metabolic causes (thyroid, B12, Wilson disease)
Assess levodopa responsiveness to differentiate PD from atypical parkinsonism
Evaluate orthostatic hypotension and consider contribution of beta-blocker before attributing it to autonomic failure
Screen for cognitive impairment and characterize its pattern
Check for drug interactions before starting dopaminergic therapy
contraindicated_actions: Do not prescribe dopamine agonists as first-line monotherapy in a 69-year-old with cognitive impairment (risk of hallucinations and impulse control disorders)
Do not prescribe anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl, benztropine) given patient's age and cognitive impairment
Do not start non-selective MAO inhibitors with current SSRI (fluoxetine) due to serotonin syndrome risk
Do not discontinue metoprolol abruptly without taper
Do not diagnose MSA based solely on orthostatic hypotension without considering the contribution of beta-blocker therapy
key_reasoning_points: Asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity form the cardinal motor triad of Parkinson's disease
Normal brain MRI without cerebellar, pontine, or midbrain atrophy helps exclude MSA and PSP
Orthostatic hypotension is common in PD (30-58% prevalence) and should not automatically trigger an MSA diagnosis, especially when a patient is on a beta-blocker (metoprolol) that can exacerbate it
Positive levodopa challenge (>30% UPDRS improvement) strongly supports PD over atypical parkinsonism
MoCA score of 22 with frontal-subcortical pattern suggests PD-MCI rather than Alzheimer's disease
Pisa syndrome (lateral trunk flexion) can occur in PD, not exclusively in atypical parkinsonism
The self-mutilating behavior described in the source case is atypical and would be an unusual feature in straightforward PD, but the overall motor syndrome and levodopa response define the diagnosis
DaTscan confirms presynaptic dopaminergic deficit but does not differentiate PD from MSA or PSP; clinical features and levodopa response are needed for this distinction
Wide-based gait and early falls warrant consideration of PSP, but absence of vertical gaze palsy and presence of prominent tremor argue against it

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, and rigidity. Orthostatic hypotension and cognitive impairment provide some clinical complexity but do not obscure the core diagnosis. MRI is unremarkable for structural or atypical parkinsonian features. Levodopa challenge is positive.
